ocr.text: In the ongoing pursuit of understanding the differential impact of biomarkers in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
(NMOSD), the data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of various modeling approaches employed to
assess the significance of specific predictors among patient cohorts. The table delineates the outcomes of conventional
regression analyses, focusing on the variable of serum analysis as a predictor.
Method  Predictor of the model  Basis type  Type of model
M1  X  None  Conventional regression
M2  W  B-splines  Functional regression (Eq. 8)
M3  W  Fourier  Functional regression (Eq. 8)
M4  X*  B-splines  Alternative 1 for Functional regression (Eq. 9)
M5  X*  Fourier  Alternative 1 for Functional regression (Eq. 9)
M6  X**  B-splines  Alternative 2 for Functional regression (Eq. 10)
M7  X**  Fourier  Alternative 2 for Functional regression (Eq. 10)
Variable  AQP4(−) patients (n=16)  AQP4(+) patients (n=53)  P values
Serum analysis
Notably, the patient groups are categorized based on the presence of aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies, with AQP4(−) patients
comprising 16 individuals and AQP4(+) patients encompassing 53 individuals. The findings underscore the distinct biochemical
profiles that may contribute to differing clinical manifestations and disease progression in these subgroups. Moreover, the
column detailing P values serves to highlight the statistical significance of the relationships observed, offering crucial insights
into the predictive validity of the models employed. This analysis not only enhances our understanding of NMOSD
pathophysiology but also underscores the importance of tailored therapeutic strategies that consider the underlying
immunological differences between AQP4(−) and AQP4(+) patients. Such insights are imperative for advancing personalized
medicine approaches in the management of this complex neurological disorder.
